design_philosophy.core_loop: Move and shoot in eight-way directions while managing ammunition pickups, rescuing hostages, and defeating waves of enemy soldiers across island raid stages
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing offensive firepower against resource scarcity and hostage protection (score penalty for collateral damage)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Revolutionary commando executing a raid on a Caribbean island, commandeering vehicles and heavy weapons to liberate territory from a dictator's forces
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Heavy emphasis on two-player cooperative simultaneous action with unlimited continues, reducing arcade quarter-eating frustration. NES version deliberately softened difficulty compared to arcade to be more accessible. Game does not shy from its revolutionary Cuban context in Japanese version but obfuscates it internationally through localization.

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives with health per life
difficulty.continues_system: Unlimited continues (major deviation from arcade, NES-specific design choice)
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression with level/difficulty select menu available via cheat code
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Hidden level and difficulty select accessible via A+B button combination during player selection, allowing players to skip early content or adjust challenge

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon pickups from defeated enemies (different gun types and equipment found in stages), tank/vehicle piloting mechanics
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage progression unlocks subsequent areas; all stages except final area accessible via hidden difficulty menu
replayability_hooks: Two-player simultaneous cooperative play with different character mechanics
Hidden minigame (Sasuke vs. Commander in Japanese version) unlockable via cheat code
Score attack potential with hostage rescue scoring (points reduced for hostage deaths)
Different weapon types to experiment with across runs

world_building_techniques: Stage themes progress geographically through island (map screen shows progression)
Environmental hazards and enemy placements suggest military fortifications
Minecart sequence implies industrial infrastructure of island
Final boss confrontation with Dictator character provides narrative culmination
Hostage rescue mechanic reinforces liberation narrative without explicit dialogue

boss_design.philosophy: Escalating mechanical challenge with environment-based encounters. Final boss features multi-phase design with turret destruction preceding melee combat.
notable_bosses: Batista (Dictator) - final boss with roof turret phase followed by direct combat; unused graphics show fleeing animation suggesting scrapped first phase
Plow Brothers - encountered in minecart section, apparently designed to interact with cart mechanics
Turret formations - 4-turret blocking sequences on final stage (possibly replaced larger tank mini-boss)

faction_indicators: Player characters as revolutionary commandos (Guevara/Castro in JP, unnamed rebels in international)
Hostages as civilian population under dictatorship
Dictator Batista as authoritarian regime representative
danger_signals: International localization explicitly removes character names and revolutionary context to avoid 'anti-Communist sentiments in the West'
Title changed from 'Guevara' to 'Guerrilla War' for Western markets
Epilogue included in Japanese version but status unclear in international releases
Factions and revolutionary messaging suppressed rather than eliminated
environmental_storytelling: Island map progression suggests military occupation geography
Minecart/industrial section implies colonial resource extraction infrastructure
Barracks, turret placements, and fortified positions suggest military regime structures
Hostage presence in combat zones emphasizes civilian oppression narrative

sources_used: tcrf - Guerrilla War (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor (unused graphics, hidden features, regional differences, developer commentary)
wikipedia - Guerrilla War (video game) (release dates, developer credits, plot summary, localization context, reception)
